---
title: "Geopolitical Risk, Internationalization, and Firm Performance among European SMEs"
subtitle: "A Panel Data Analysis"
author: "Jonas Pamminger | ExInt II | WU Vienna | SS 2026 | [github.com/WuStudent0/Repo-Masterarbeit-Jonas-Pamminger](https://github.com/WuStudent0/Repo-Masterarbeit-Jonas-Pamminger)"
date: "2026-06-25"
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    papersize: a4
    fontsize: 11pt
    geometry:
      - margin=2.5cm
    number-sections: true
    toc: false
    colorlinks: true
    cite-method: citeproc
bibliography: references/library.bib
csl: references/apa.csl
jupyter: sme-internationalization
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import statsmodels.api as sm
from linearmodels.panel import PanelOLS

plt.style.use("seaborn-v0_8-whitegrid")
plt.rcParams.update({"figure.dpi": 150, "font.size": 9})

df = pd.read_parquet("data/processed/panel_clean.parquet")
for c in df.select_dtypes(include=["Float64"]).columns:
    df[c] = df[c].astype("float64")

df["doi_x_size"] = df["doi"] * df["ln_at"]
```

# Introduction

Geopolitical tensions have escalated dramatically in the past decade, fundamentally reshaping the environment in which firms conduct international business. The Russian invasion of Ukraine in 2022 and the subsequent imposition of comprehensive Western sanctions led more than 1,000 foreign firms to suspend or terminate their Russian operations [@balyuk2023divesting], while escalating technology decoupling between the United States and China has further fragmented global markets [@luo2023geostrategic; @witt2023decoupling]. These developments confront multinational enterprises (MNEs) and internationally active small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) with a novel risk type: state-imposed sanctions and geopolitical disruptions that can force market withdrawals or necessitate costly strategic reorientations [@moura2025mnes; @witt2019deglobalization].

Against this backdrop, the question of how internationalization affects firm performance acquires new urgency. If the short-run performance costs of maintaining foreign operations are substantial --- as the liability of foreignness framework predicts [@zaheer1995overcoming] --- then geopolitically induced de-internationalization may paradoxically improve accounting performance in the short run, even as it destroys long-run strategic value. Understanding the magnitude of the internationalization--performance penalty, and the conditions under which it is attenuated, is therefore critical for both managers navigating de-internationalization decisions under sanctions pressure and policymakers designing effective sanctions regimes [@hufbauer2007sanctions; @kalinowski2019deinternationalization].

The existing literature on internationalization and firm performance is characterised by two important gaps in the European SME context. First, causal identification of the performance effect is typically confounded by unobserved firm heterogeneity: firms that internationalise differ systematically in management quality, strategic orientation, and institutional capabilities --- characteristics that independently affect performance [@hausman1978specification]. Second, the boundary conditions of the internationalization--performance relationship remain insufficiently theorised. In particular, whether firm size --- which governs the resource base available to manage complex foreign operations and to navigate institutional disruptions --- meaningfully moderates the performance effect has received limited systematic attention [@cohen1990absorptive; @contractor2003curvilinear].

This research note addresses both gaps using a panel of European SMEs drawn from Compustat Global over fiscal years 2005--2020. Employing two-way fixed effects (TWFE) estimation with firm- and year-level controls, we ask two related questions: does the degree of internationalization (DOI) exert a negative effect on current-period return on assets (RoA); and does firm size moderate this relationship by enabling larger SMEs to manage international operations more efficiently?

**Research question:** Does the degree of internationalization affect firm performance among European SMEs, and does firm size moderate this relationship?

# Theoretical Background and Hypotheses

## The OLI Paradigm and the Costs of Internationalization

The eclectic (OLI) paradigm [@dunning1980eclectic; @dunning2001eclectic] provides the foundational framework for understanding when internationalization creates value. According to the OLI logic, foreign market engagement is profitable when firms simultaneously possess ownership advantages (firm-specific competitive assets such as technology or brand), can access location advantages (host-country factors including market size, resource availability, and institutional stability), and benefit from internalising cross-border transactions within the firm hierarchy [@buckley1976future; @rugman2008internalization].

In the context of geopolitical risk, the OLI framework predicts that sanctions erode location advantages by restricting market access, interrupting trade and capital flows, and destabilising the institutional environment of target markets [@hufbauer2007sanctions]. When location advantages deteriorate, the OLI balance tips against maintaining foreign operations, and de-internationalization becomes the rational strategic response [@benito2005divestment; @dunning2008multinational]. For SMEs, ownership advantages that could compensate for eroding location advantages --- managerial expertise, proprietary technology, political connections --- are typically more limited than for large MNEs, making them particularly vulnerable to the performance costs of international exposure.

The Uppsala model [@johanson1977internationalization] adds a temporal dimension: internationalization is a gradual process of experiential learning, implying that firms in early or intermediate stages bear disproportionate costs relative to their accumulated foreign market knowledge. The liability of foreignness framework [@zaheer1995overcoming; @kostova1999legitimacy] identifies the specific cost mechanisms --- coordination costs, information asymmetries, and legitimacy deficits --- that depress performance for internationally active firms. For European SMEs operating across institutionally diverse environments, these costs are compounded by regulatory heterogeneity and the growing complexity of geopolitical compliance [@meyer2022mnes; @petricevic2019structural].

Together, these theoretical perspectives converge on a clear prediction for the short-run performance effect of internationalization among resource-constrained SMEs.

**H1:** The degree of internationalization negatively affects current-period RoA among European SMEs, reflecting the coordination costs and liability of foreignness associated with international operations.

*Test:* $\beta(\text{doi}) < 0$ and statistically significant

## The Moderating Role of Firm Size

Institutional theory [@north1990institutions; @scott1995institutions] provides a complementary lens for understanding heterogeneous responses to the challenges of internationalization. Firms differ in their capacity to navigate complex institutional environments --- a capability that @dunning2008multinational term "institutional ownership advantages." Larger SMEs, by virtue of their more extensive human capital base, more differentiated functional expertise, and greater financial slack, are better positioned to manage institutional complexity without sustaining disproportionate performance losses [@lu2001internationalization].

The absorptive capacity framework [@cohen1990absorptive] formalises this intuition. Absorptive capacity --- the ability to recognise, assimilate, and exploit new knowledge --- is a function of prior cumulative investment in knowledge-generating activities and organisational breadth. The mechanism operates through two channels. First, a *scale effect*: larger SMEs can spread the fixed costs of international operations --- legal compliance, market intelligence, sanctions screening --- over a larger asset and revenue base, reducing the per-unit earnings impact of any given level of internationalization. Second, a *complementarity effect*: larger SMEs possess more developed downstream capabilities that accelerate the conversion of foreign market presence into revenues, shortening the effective lag between international expansion and performance payoff [@contractor2003curvilinear; @hitt1997international].

The real options perspective [@dixit1994investment; @kogut1991joint] further supports this moderation: larger firms possess greater flexibility to reconfigure their international portfolios in response to geopolitical shocks --- maintaining, scaling back, or exiting foreign operations as conditions evolve --- while smaller SMEs face more binary and costly adjustment paths [@zamborsky2023resilience].

**H2:** Firm size positively moderates the DOI--RoA relationship --- larger SMEs experience a smaller performance penalty from internationalization than their smaller counterparts.

*Test:* $\beta(\text{doi} \times \text{ln\_at}) > 0$

# Data and Method

## Sample

The empirical analysis draws on annual firm-level data from the WRDS Compustat Global database (`comp_global_daily.g_funda`), which provides standardised financial statement data for listed firms in over 80 countries. The sample is restricted to firms incorporated in 23 European countries that satisfy the EU SME definition of either fewer than 250 employees or total assets not exceeding EUR 43 million, over fiscal years 2005--2020. We require positive total assets and positive sales, and a minimum of three firm-year observations to support within-firm identification. All continuous variables except the logarithmic size measure are winsorized at the 1st and 99th percentiles to mitigate extreme outliers [@white1980heteroskedasticity]. The resulting unbalanced panel comprises 29,344 firm-years from 3,843 unique firms across 23 European countries.

## Variables

**Dependent variable.** Firm performance is operationalised as return on assets (RoA), calculated as income before extraordinary items divided by total assets (`ib / at`). RoA is the most widely used accounting-based performance measure in the SME panel-data literature, providing a scale-invariant assessment of asset productivity [@lu2001internationalization].

**Independent variable.** The degree of internationalization (DOI) is measured as pre-tax income from foreign operations divided by total sales (`fopo / sale`). This captures the extent to which a firm's earnings are generated outside its home market. Approximately 95% of firm-years in the final sample have non-zero DOI values.

**Moderator.** Firm size is measured as the natural logarithm of total assets (`log(at)`), consistent with the standard transformation for right-skewed size distributions [@lu2001internationalization].

**Control variables.** We include three time-varying controls. Leverage (`(dltt + dlc) / seq`) captures capital structure effects on profitability. Capital expenditure intensity (`capx / at`) controls for tangible investment that may independently shape RoA. R&D intensity (`xrd / at`, with missing values set to zero) proxies for innovation investment that correlates with both internationalization and performance [@cohen1990absorptive]. @tbl-variables provides a complete variable overview.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-variables
#| tbl-cap: "Variable definitions and data sources."

var_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Variable": ["RoA", "DOI", "DOI x Size", "Firm size", "Leverage",
                  "CAPX intensity", "R&D intensity"],
    "Field(s)": ["ib, at", "fopo, sale", "---", "at", "dltt, dlc, seq",
                  "capx, at", "xrd, at"],
    "Formula": ["ib / at", "fopo / sale", "doi x ln_at", "log(at)",
                 "(dltt+dlc) / seq", "capx / at", "xrd.fillna(0) / at"],
    "Role": ["Dependent (Y)", "Independent (X)", "H2 interaction",
             "Moderator + Control", "Control", "Control", "Control"]
})

print(var_df.to_markdown(index=False))
```

## Estimation Strategy

We estimate a two-way fixed effects (TWFE) panel model of the form:

$$\text{RoA}_{it} = \beta_1 \text{DOI}_{it} + \beta_2 (\text{DOI} \times \text{Size})_{it} + \gamma X_{it} + \mu_i + \lambda_t + \varepsilon_{it}$$

where $\mu_i$ are firm fixed effects absorbing all time-invariant firm-level heterogeneity --- including country of domicile, industry affiliation, management quality, and organisational culture --- and $\lambda_t$ are year fixed effects absorbing common macroeconomic shocks such as the 2008--09 financial crisis, the European debt crisis, and rising geopolitical tensions. Standard errors are clustered at the firm level to account for within-firm serial correlation [@petersen2009estimating]. The choice of fixed over random effects is supported by the divergence between OLS and FE estimates, consistent with correlation between firm-level unobservables and DOI [@hausman1978specification]. All analyses use the `linearmodels` Python package [@trisovic2022large].

# Results

## Descriptive Statistics

```{python}
#| label: tbl-descriptives
#| tbl-cap: "Descriptive statistics. Continuous variables winsorized at 1st-99th percentiles."

desc_cols = ["roa", "doi", "rd_intensity", "ln_at", "leverage", "capex_intensity"]
labels = {"roa": "RoA", "doi": "DOI", "rd_intensity": "R&D Intensity",
          "ln_at": "Firm Size (log)", "leverage": "Leverage",
          "capex_intensity": "CAPX Intensity"}

desc = df[desc_cols].describe().T[["count", "mean", "std", "min", "50%", "max"]]
desc.columns = ["N", "mean", "std", "min", "Median", "max"]
desc.index = [labels[c] for c in desc_cols]
desc["N"] = desc["N"].astype(int)

for c in ["mean", "std", "min", "Median", "max"]:
    desc[c] = desc[c].map(lambda x: f"{x:.3f}")

print(desc.to_markdown())
```

@tbl-descriptives presents descriptive statistics for all research variables. The distribution of RoA exhibits the characteristic left-skew observed in SME panel data: the median is close to zero while the mean is pulled below by loss-making firms. The DOI distribution has a mean of 0.337, with approximately 67% of firm-years showing positive foreign income and 28% showing negative foreign income (losses from foreign operations). The correlation matrix (@fig-corr) reveals a negative raw correlation between DOI and RoA, providing preliminary descriptive support for H1.

```{python}
#| label: fig-scatter
#| fig-cap: "DOI versus RoA for firms with non-zero DOI (left panel, with bin means in red) and median RoA by firm size for firms with and without foreign operations (right panel). The right panel illustrates the H2 moderation pattern: the performance gap between internationally active and domestic firms narrows with firm size."

fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4))

active = df[df["doi"] != 0].copy()
ax1.scatter(active["doi"], active["roa"], alpha=0.03, s=3, color="steelblue")
bins = pd.cut(active["doi"], bins=20)
bin_means = active.groupby(bins, observed=True)["roa"].mean()
bin_centers = [(b.left + b.right) / 2 for b in bin_means.index]
ax1.scatter(bin_centers, bin_means.values, color="red", s=40, zorder=5, label="Bin means")
ax1.set_xlabel("DOI (foreign income / sales)")
ax1.set_ylabel("RoA")
ax1.set_title("DOI vs RoA (active firms)")
ax1.set_xlim(-2, 3)
ax1.set_ylim(-1.5, 1)
ax1.legend()

df_plot = df.copy()
df_plot["has_foreign"] = np.where(df_plot["doi"] != 0, "With foreign ops", "Domestic only")
df_plot["size_q"] = pd.qcut(df_plot["ln_at"], 5, labels=["Q1\n(smallest)", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4", "Q5\n(largest)"])
medians = df_plot.groupby(["size_q", "has_foreign"], observed=True)["roa"].median().unstack()
medians.plot(kind="bar", ax=ax2, color=["steelblue", "coral"])
ax2.set_xlabel("Firm Size Quintile")
ax2.set_ylabel("Median RoA")
ax2.set_title("RoA by Size: Foreign vs Domestic")
ax2.legend(title="")
ax2.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=0)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("output/fig_scatter.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-corr
#| fig-cap: "Pairwise correlation matrix for research variables. No pair of independent variables exceeds a correlation of |0.35|, indicating that multicollinearity is unlikely to distort coefficient estimates."

corr_cols = ["roa", "doi", "rd_intensity", "ln_at", "leverage", "capex_intensity"]
corr_labels = ["RoA", "DOI", "R&D Int.", "Firm Size", "Leverage", "CAPX Int."]

corr = df[corr_cols].corr()
corr.index = corr_labels
corr.columns = corr_labels

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 5))
mask = np.triu(np.ones_like(corr, dtype=bool), k=1)
sns.heatmap(corr, mask=mask, annot=True, fmt=".2f", cmap="RdBu_r",
            center=0, vmin=-1, vmax=1, square=True, linewidths=0.5, ax=ax)
ax.set_title("Correlation Matrix")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("output/fig_corr.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()
```

## Regression Results

```{python}
#| label: tbl-regression
#| tbl-cap: "Panel regression results. Dependent variable: RoA. Standard errors in parentheses, clustered at firm level in Models (2)-(3). * p<0.10, ** p<0.05, *** p<0.01."

panel = df.set_index(["gvkey", "fyear"])

y = panel["roa"]
X_ols = sm.add_constant(panel[["doi", "ln_at", "leverage", "capex_intensity", "rd_intensity"]])
m1 = sm.OLS(y, X_ols).fit(cov_type="HC1")

exog2 = panel[["doi", "ln_at", "leverage", "capex_intensity", "rd_intensity"]]
m2 = PanelOLS(panel["roa"], exog2, entity_effects=True, time_effects=True).fit(
    cov_type="clustered", cluster_entity=True)

exog3 = panel[["doi", "doi_x_size", "ln_at", "leverage", "capex_intensity", "rd_intensity"]]
m3 = PanelOLS(panel["roa"], exog3, entity_effects=True, time_effects=True).fit(
    cov_type="clustered", cluster_entity=True)

def stars(p):
    if p < 0.01: return "***"
    elif p < 0.05: return "**"
    elif p < 0.10: return "*"
    return ""

def fmt(coef, se, pval):
    return f"{coef:.4f}{stars(pval)}", f"({se:.4f})"

vars_order = ["doi", "doi_x_size", "ln_at", "leverage", "capex_intensity", "rd_intensity"]
var_labels = {"doi": "doi", "doi_x_size": "doi_x_size", "ln_at": "ln_at",
              "leverage": "leverage", "capex_intensity": "capx_intensity",
              "rd_intensity": "rd_intensity"}

rows = []
for v in vars_order:
    row_coef = [var_labels[v]]
    row_se = [""]
    for m_obj, is_ols in [(m1, True), (m2, False), (m3, False)]:
        if is_ols:
            params, ses, pvals = m_obj.params, m_obj.bse, m_obj.pvalues
        else:
            params, ses, pvals = m_obj.params, m_obj.std_errors, m_obj.pvalues
        if v in params.index:
            c, s = fmt(params[v], ses[v], pvals[v])
            row_coef.append(c)
            row_se.append(s)
        else:
            row_coef.append("")
            row_se.append("")
    rows.append(row_coef)
    rows.append(row_se)

rows.append(["Firm FE", "No", "Yes", "Yes"])
rows.append(["Year FE", "No", "Yes", "Yes"])
rows.append(["Clustered SE", "No", "Yes", "Yes"])
rows.append(["N", f"{int(m1.nobs):,}", f"{int(m2.nobs):,}", f"{int(m3.nobs):,}"])
rows.append(["R-squared", f"{m1.rsquared:.3f}", f"{m2.rsquared_within:.3f}", f"{m3.rsquared_within:.3f}"])

header = ["Variable", "(1) OLS", "(2) TWFE", "(3) TWFE+H2"]
result_df = pd.DataFrame(rows, columns=header)
print(result_df.to_markdown(index=False))
```

@tbl-regression presents results from three specifications: pooled OLS with heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors (Model 1), two-way fixed effects with firm-clustered standard errors (Model 2), and the TWFE specification augmented with the DOI x firm size interaction term (Model 3).

**H1 --- DOI and RoA.** DOI enters with a negative and statistically significant coefficient across all three specifications. The main TWFE estimate (Model 2) is $\hat{\beta}_1 = -0.029$ (p < 0.01), indicating that a one-unit increase in DOI is associated with a reduction in within-firm RoA of approximately 0.029 units in the same fiscal year, holding all other covariates constant. **H1 is supported.** This finding is consistent with the liability of foreignness mechanism [@zaheer1995overcoming]: the costs of coordinating foreign operations --- regulatory compliance, cultural adaptation, sanctions screening, and information asymmetry --- reduce short-run accounting profitability for European SMEs.

The comparison between the OLS estimate ($\hat{\beta}_1^{\text{OLS}} = -0.039$) and the TWFE estimate is instructive. The OLS coefficient exceeds the TWFE coefficient in absolute magnitude by approximately 33%, consistent with the presence of omitted variable bias in the cross-sectional estimator. The fixed effects estimator eliminates this confound by exploiting only within-firm variation over time [@hausman1978specification].

**H2 --- Firm size as moderator.** The interaction term between DOI and the logarithm of total assets (Model 3) carries a positive and statistically significant coefficient ($\hat{\beta}_2 = 0.008$, p < 0.01). **H2 is supported.** This indicates that larger SMEs experience a significantly smaller performance penalty from internationalization than their smaller counterparts. Evaluated at the mean firm size (ln_at = 2.96), the marginal effect of DOI on RoA is $-0.050 + 0.008 \times 2.96 = -0.027$, while for firms one standard deviation above the mean (ln_at = 4.48), the marginal effect is $-0.050 + 0.008 \times 4.48 = -0.014$ --- approximately half the penalty faced by the average firm. This is consistent with both the absorptive capacity mechanism [@cohen1990absorptive] and the real options perspective [@dixit1994investment]: larger SMEs possess the organisational infrastructure to manage the costs of internationalization and the flexibility to reconfigure their international portfolios in response to geopolitical disruptions.

# Discussion and Conclusion

## Discussion

The central finding of this research note --- that DOI exerts a negative and statistically robust effect on current-period RoA among European SMEs --- has direct implications for understanding de-internationalization under geopolitical pressure. If maintaining foreign operations imposes a measurable short-run performance penalty, then sanctions-induced withdrawal from foreign markets may paradoxically improve accounting profitability in the short run, even as it destroys the long-run strategic value associated with international diversification and knowledge accumulation [@johanson1977internationalization; @dunning2001eclectic].

This interpretation connects to the broader literature on MNE responses to geopolitical shocks. @balyuk2023divesting document that firms divesting from Russia after 2022 experienced short-run stock price increases, consistent with investors pricing in the elimination of the foreign operations penalty that our within-firm estimates quantify. @moura2025mnes argue that geopolitical risk fundamentally alters the cost--benefit calculus of internationalization, making the OLI framework's location advantages more volatile and harder to predict. Our findings provide empirical grounding for this argument: with a TWFE-estimated DOI penalty of $-0.029$ on RoA, the costs of maintaining foreign operations are non-trivial and may tip the OLI balance toward de-internationalization when geopolitical conditions deteriorate.

The significant positive moderation by firm size extends this interpretation in a policy-relevant direction. Smaller SMEs bear a disproportionately large performance penalty from internationalization, consistent with institutional theory's prediction that navigating complex regulatory environments requires organisational capabilities that scale with firm size [@north1990institutions; @scott1995institutions]. This implies that sanctions and geopolitical disruptions have heterogeneous effects across the firm size distribution: smaller, less resourced firms face stronger incentives to de-internationalise when geopolitical conditions worsen, potentially leading to a concentration of international activity among larger firms --- a pattern @evenett2023western document in the post-2022 Russia context, where smaller firms exited disproportionately relative to large multinationals.

## Conclusion

This research note examines the short-run effect of internationalization on firm performance among European SMEs using a panel of 29,344 firm-years from 3,843 firms across 23 European countries over 2005--2020. The evidence strongly supports H1: within the same firm, years characterised by higher DOI are associated with significantly lower RoA ($\hat{\beta} = -0.029$, p < 0.01), with the magnitude of the effect attenuated by 33% relative to the naive OLS estimate once firm fixed effects are controlled. H2 is also supported: firm size positively and significantly moderates this relationship ($\hat{\beta}_2 = 0.008$, p < 0.01), indicating that larger SMEs face approximately half the performance penalty of their smaller counterparts.

These findings contribute to the emerging literature on geopolitical risk and de-internationalization [@witt2019deglobalization; @witt2023decoupling] by quantifying the short-run performance cost of international exposure that makes de-internationalization a rational response to sanctions pressure. Three limitations bound the scope of these conclusions. First, DOI measured as foreign income share captures only one dimension of internationalization; alternative measures based on foreign assets or geographic dispersion may yield different results. Second, the observation window (2005--2020) predates the most severe sanctions episodes of 2022, limiting direct inference about sanctions-specific effects. Third, Compustat Global coverage skews toward listed firms; generalisation to the private SME population requires caution. Future research could extend this analysis by exploiting quasi-experimental variation in EU sanctions regimes as instruments for exogenous shocks to internationalization intensity, or by examining the post-2022 period as a natural experiment in forced de-internationalization [@balyuk2023divesting; @zamborsky2023resilience].

# References
